Description: Flagged because they usually mean the monitor was not recording normally (uninstall, battery/network issues, or missed logs), so the time series is not continuous and needs an explanation.
Flagged because near-zero DO readings can either indicate a probe issue (out of water, bio-fouling) or an extreme low-dissolved-oxygen event that should be verified.
Flagged because DO typically does not change that sharply over 15 minutes unless something external happened (probe moved/cleaned/reset or exposed to air).
Flagged because very high DO early in the day can be sensor noise. It is an outlier that can change interpretation if not reviewed.
Flagged because these pH values are unusual for pond aquaculture and could point to measurement error or an exceptional event.
Flagged because a 1-unit jump in 15 minutes for pH could be linked with probe disturbance/reset or recording issues rather than a true pond change.
Flagged because they are the single best-aligned measurements to satellite overpass timing, so we can compare ground truth to satellite across ponds and dates.

Flag_Name: The conditional formatting colors may override each other and only one color will be displayed for cells that have multiple flags.

Description: Additional note: Some readings contain exact zero values (0) for DO, pH, and/or Temperature. These are not flagged by the conditional formatting above but are equipment artifacts — typically caused by device resets, sensor initialization on startup, or outages — and do not represent real water quality measurements. Most occur on January 8 2026 across many devices simultaneously (likely a coordinated calibration or firmware event). A few ponds also have longer stretches of zeros due to device outages or harvest-related uninstallation. These zero-value readings should be filtered out before analysis.
